When we think about the power of reciprocity, we tend to focus in on how it works at the individual level. And it's certainly true that reciprocity does operate between individuals. But its impact and power go far beyond this initial level. Reciprocity comes into play between organizations as well. For example, when companies exchange favors with their business partners. Reciprocity can also bind entire societies together. And it can do so over time across generations. Think of the example I shared where the city of Columbia, South Carolina donated a fire truck worth $354,000 to the city of New York after 9-11-2001. Which they did because New Yorkers had given them a gift of a fire carriage a century prior after the ravages of the Civil War. The citizens of New York who received the fire truck in 2001 were certainly not the same individuals who had given one to the citizens of Columbia a hundred years before. Yet the rule for reciprocation was still operating forcefully, despite differences in the people, places and eras involved. That's how powerful it is.
